table_of_content.title: Introduction
text: Currently, logistics management is one of the important factors for the company's value chain. Proper supply chain management makes it possible not only to quickly deliver products from point A to point B, but also makes it possible to optimize costs even in a turbulent external environment.

Warehousing services are one of the most important elements of the supply chain. They allow not only to implement the storage of the necessary stocks formed in the production process and distribution, but rationally manage the outgoing flow throughout the supply chain. Effective management of warehouse business processes using automatization and robotization can significantly reduce company costs and achieve competitive advantage due to the high efficiency of storage facilities. “Automation” is the process or act of turning the control of a device or machine into a more automatic system. Generally, “automatization” has the same meaning. Robotization is the displacement of people from the productive process, with their replacement by automated and robotic machines and production lines, in connection with which resources are released for the development of the service sector.

The chosen format of the final qualification work is a consulting project for Unilever. The relevance of the topic is explained by the increase in the wages of employees by 55% due to the company's policy and the functional problem of the low productivity of industrial transport. For 2022, the company increased the salaries of employees by 55%, which significantly affected both the company's costs and warehouse logistics. Unilever is facing the need to increase productivity in the warehouse and the company's strategy for 2025 is to use more robotic vehicles, even company employees admit that there are more technologically advanced warehouse vehicles on the market that will increase productivity.
The managerial problem is the need to introduce robotic and automated solutions in the supply chain at the Unilever Rus safe storage warehouse (ice cream product).

The main goal of the thesis is to propose robotic and automated solutions, determining weak zones of warehouse business process to increase capability of floor industrial transport for Unilever Rus ice cream division.
To achieve this goal, it is necessary sequential solution of the following tasks:
Analytical review of the situation in the warehouse;
Industry analysis of the ice cream industry in Russia;
Analysis of the current performance of Unilever Rus in the field of sustainable development through the analysis of the value chain;
Determination the points in the business processes of the warehouse using the analysis of the value chain and business processes;
Offering technical problem solving capabilities;
Economic evaluation of recommendations.

The object of the thesis is the Unilever Rus company (IC division), the subject is robotization and automation of logistics. The work consists of three chapters: the first is devoted to the analysis of the current activities of the company, as well as the analysis of the microenvironment. For their implementation, the author will use Porter's 5 Forces frameworks. The second chapter is devoted to the analysis of the value chain and warehouse business processes to identify weak areas in the process of operations and outbound logistics. Relevant technological solutions will be proposed in accordance with the conclusions. The third chapter is devoted to recommendations and a miscalculation from payback.

The article deals with both primary and secondary data sources. Primary data refer to interviews with company representatives. Secondary data includes documents and books related to the subject of research (MarketLine, Springer) and other data.
The proposed toolkit, which allows solving the problem:
value chain concept by Michal Porter;
Porter's 5 Five analysis;
Analytical formulas for calculating the need for personnel;
Analytical formulas for calculating the need for technological equipment.

table_of_content.title: CHAPTER 1. COMPANY AND INDUSTRY ANALYSIS
text: This chapter examines the activities of Unilever Rus. as well as its current performance. This will also include a market overview, a macro-environmental study, and a competitive analysis. The following tools will be used in the research: Porter's Five Forces analysis. Porter's Five Forces model aids in analyzing industry rivalry and factors that influence its attractiveness.

sub_elements.title: 1.1. Brief history, current state of the company and its structure
text: Unilever entered the Russian market in 1991, with the opening of the first office in Moscow in 1992, representing "UniRus." They began by importing only the Lux, Omo, Signal, Impulse, and Lipton brands.

In 1994, Unilever established its Russian headquarters in St. Petersburg, and by July 1995, the company had acquired its first plant in Russia, Severnoye Siyaniye. Unilever, on the other hand, initially concentrated on chemical products and established the Russian office of Van den Bergen Jurgens B.V. in 1996 to expand into the food and beverage sector. Rama, Delmi, Calve, and Brook Bond were introduced to the Russian market during this period. However, they were all imported decades ago. Calve goods originally arrived in Russia in 1996, but were imported from the Netherlands and distributed by UniRus until 1998. By 1998, these entirely imported commodities had increased to 7% of the market. (Unilever, 2008) Imports were halted in the aftermath of the 1998 financial crisis. Calve mayonnaise manufacture was developed in Russia in 1999. The brand's market share has continuously increased since 2001, when it entered Ukraine; the same year, Calve ketchup was produced; and in 2003, Calve sauces were introduced (History of Brenda Calve 2005). The corporation's operations in Russia have continued to grow. While Unilever built its own facilities, the majority of its expansion was accomplished through acquisitions of local players. As a result, Unilever remained true to its headquarters development strategy while mitigating the risks associated with Greenfield initiatives in industrialized countries. Unilever agreed in 2009 to acquire the sauce business of a Russian company, Baltimor. Baltimor is Russia's largest manufacturer of ketchup. Baltimor began operations in late 1990" in Saint Petersburg (and was first meant to serve only this region), but quickly recognized the product's promise was far broader and began spreading throughout Russia. Baltimor entered the market ahead of big multinational competitors, utilizing an aggressive marketing strategy and establishing connections with distributors to take more than half of the Russian ketchup market by the year 2000. The business began to grow when it acquired an agricultural estate in southern Russia in 2004 and began producing canned veggies. Despite the fact that the new business was extremely profitable, accounting for 25% of Baltimor's earnings from 2005 to 2007, Baltimor has invested extensively in business growth, resulting in the company's net debt reaching 90 million dollars. Simultaneously, the founders of the company agreed to create their own butter factory in Krasnodar. The estimated cost of the factory was $12 million; the actual cost was $25 million. The firm was compelled to implement cost-cutting measures as a result of difficulty refinancing debt. One of these milestones was the conclusion of the arrangement with the 3PL, with whom the ketchup division was affiliated. None of the initiatives, however, were sufficiently bold, and Unilever acquired Baltimor's most valuable and successful company, the ketchup group, in 2009.

The company's worldwide management consistently emphasizes the critical nature of the Russian division's and the Russian economy's development. This strategy contributes to the company's overall strength. Unilever was placed 34th in Forbes magazine's 2015 ranking of the "50 largest foreign firms in Russia," and 27th in 2016.

This breadth of brands necessitates that the company's management pay special attention to the organizational structure. To ensure that the business operates well in today's market conditions and is capable of achieving its objectives, the management frequently revises the organizational structure of departments and the relationships between them. All of these adjustments at Unilever Russia are aimed at ensuring that the company can operate in the following seven functional areas invariably:
1.the marketing function is responsible for the company's brand development and product range formation;
2.the supply chain management function is responsible for the planning, procurement, production, transportation, warehousing, and quality assurance of finished products;
3.the sales function is responsible for ensuring that products are available on store shelves throughout Russia, Belarus, and Ukraine by establishing and servicing a customer base;
4.the financial function is responsible for the company's financial health.
5. the function of information technology, contributing to the introduction of the latest management systems and providing technical support for all other functions;

6. the personnel management function, which is responsible for recruiting and developing employees, paying compensation, resolving administrative issues, and resolving internal communication problems;
7. the corporate communications function, which is responsible for interacting with government agencies and the media.
Structure of Unilever

Historically, the company has developed a corporate structure. This was the impetus for the development of the independence branch, which has survived to this day: the power and management of the company are divided geographically.
Strategic management is carried out mainly by the executive director, as well as directors, geographic and functional purpose.

There are five executive positions: Director of Finance, Director of Research and Development, Director of Human Resources, Director of Supply Chain, and Director of Marketing and Communications. Product division of management is carried out as follows: four directors are responsible for four main areas (care products, household chemicals and other home care products, food and refreshing products).

Group management is also divided into three main geographical segments: Americas, Western Europe, as well as a combination of segments for Africa, Asia, Central and Eastern Europe. The chief president of each segment is the regional and its Board of Registration. The above disadvantages include management features (Fig. 1).
Structure of strategic management
For further management of divisions, each segment is subdivided into smaller structures: Markets of Russia, Ukraine and Belarus (Unilever RUB).
Regional management structure

The Russian division is united into a single organizational matrix structure. The division is carried out according to the main functional areas (Customer Relations Development Department, Production Planning and Logistics Department, Marketing Department) and is associated with support departments (Finance Department, Human Resources Department, Information and Technology Department, Public Relations Department). The management structure is shown in the figure below (Fig. 2).
The management structure of the Russian division of Unilever

However, due to recent events related to the military operation on the territory of Ukraine, this structure has undergone multiple changes. At the moment, according to employees, Unilever Russia does not receive support from a global company and acts as an independent organization.

Due to the sanctions, Unilever will suspend the export of products to Russia. According to Bloomberg, the company will also stop investing in advertising and further projects in Russia. On March 8, the British company Unilever, one of the world leaders in the production of consumer goods, will suspend the export of products to the Russian Federation, as well as its import from Russia after the military operation launched by Moscow in Ukraine. Bloomberg reported this on Tuesday, citing a company representative.
The company will continue to supply vital food and hygiene products made in Russia, but will keep this issue under close scrutiny.

Thus, today the company "Unilever Rus" is the Russian division of an international company that has production, storage, transport facilities around the world, the effective functioning of which largely depends on competent logistics management and, in particular, on the management and organization of the company's storage facilities .
1.2 Impact of turbulent environment on company’s business operations

On March 8, the British company Unilever announced a halt to work in Russia due to a special operation in Ukraine. In early March, the European office of the company announced that it would suspend work in Russia. The Russian factories of the British company Unilever had to revise the range of products due to sanctions.

"Due to reduced imports of raw materials and packaging materials, our business will face a number of operational challenges. Our customers may see some changes in our product range, while the quality of products will not change." - said the representatives of the company. How exactly the enterprises will revise the assortment, the company did not specify. But they noted that factories in Yekaterinburg, Omsk, St. Petersburg and Tula will continue to produce goods. And these are personal care products, household goods and food products. Among which are the "Gold Standard"; Inmarko.

Moreover, Unilever has defined a living wage as an income level that covers a family's basic needs and "helps them break the cycle of poverty." The company has already raised salaries for its employees and wants to raise wages for people outside of its workforce to promote economic inclusion. According to Ekaterina Sokolova, Warehouse Coordinator in Tula, since the beginning of 2022, the company has increased the salaries of all employees by 55%. Wages should be enough to cover food, water, housing, education, healthcare, transportation and clothing, as well as emergency expenses, the company said.

“Unilever’s plan shows what responsible action is needed from the private sector to make an impact in the fight against inequality and help build a world where everyone has the opportunity to thrive, not just survive,” said Gabriela Bucher, chief executive of Oxfam. Oxfam is an international association fighting poverty around the world.

In addition to this, says Natalya Utkina, chief technologist of the Union of Ice Cream Workers of Russia, the period of purchase for the most profitable season for ice cream workers just fell on the growth of the dollar and the disruption of supply chains. What is more, due to the depreciation of the ruble, 20% of Russian companies including Unilever Russia have planned indexation or salary increases for staff in the near future, according to a survey by the Research Center of the SuperJob.ru portal, received on Sunday (March 6, 2022) by Interfax.

The results of the study show that two-thirds of employers (66%) have indexed or increased the wages of their employees over the past year. The survey showed that almost all employers (98%) did not delay their employees' wages for the last month. The survey was conducted on March 2-4, 2022 among 1,000 respondents representing personnel management in 174 settlements of the Russian Federation, in all federal districts.

From the above arguments, we see that the company's personnel and logistics costs have increased greatly due to the sanctions and political events. In view of this, in order to optimize intra-warehouse logistics, the company is aware of the possibility and necessity of optimizing industrial transport and increasing productivity.

sub_elements.title: 1.2 Impact of turbulent environment on company’s business operations

text: This part is dedicated to the analysis of the Russian ice cream market industry, which will be done using Porter's 5 Forces to analyze competitors.

To assess the risks of the ice cream industry in an external turbulent environment, the author proposed to carry out a Porter analysis to understand the risks. This framework will help to take into account the strengths and weaknesses of the industry and the influence of various external factors. The results of the analysis will help in the formation of possible directions for solving the problem.

Porter's Five Forces Framework is a method for assessing industry rivalry and the factors that impact it. It was introduced by Michael Porter and then published in a Harvard Business Review article. Threat of new entrants, competitive rivalry, threat of substitutes, supplier bargaining power, and buyer negotiating power are all incorporated in the instrument.

The author of this report rated each aspect on a scale of 1 to 5 (lowest threat to the industry) (the highest threat). Furthermore, each force was assessed based on a variety of industry-relevant criteria.
ANALYSIS OF THE FIVE FORCES

Ice cream companies will participate in a study of the ice cream industry. Important sellers will include supermarkets, hypermarkets, specialized merchants, and grocery stores, while key suppliers will be raw material providers (such as milk, butter, and emulsifier producers).
Power of buyers
Power of buyers
Source: MarketLine research extended by author

The most important customers in the Russian ice cream sector are convenience stores, which account for 45.2 percent of total market value. Hypermarkets and supermarkets are the second-largest distribution channel, accounting for 29.0 percent of the market's overall volume. Food retailing in Russia is fragmented, allowing players to sell to a diverse group of customers.

Ice cream manufacturers can distinguish their products based on brand, flavor, and calorie level. Large purchasers typically require a wide range of products for their customers, which reduces their purchasing power marginally. Buying power drivers are particularly weakened when people are devoted to specific brands.

Retailers are unlikely to be affected by brand loyalty, and price sensitivity remains high. Reverse integration has been attempted by several retailers; for example, supermarkets have established private label products. Large worldwide ice cream companies, on the other hand, have reacted by selling ice cream directly to customers through franchised ice cream parlors.

When it comes to exports, Russia provides a significant portion of the supply to the United States. Russian ice cream companies raised their exports to the United States by 3.7 times in the first quarter of 2021. (compared to January-March 2020). The United States has overtaken China as the world's largest buyer of Russian ice cream in terms of dollar value. Overall, the buyer power is assessed as moderate.
Power of suppliers
Power of suppliers
Source: MarketLine research extended by author

Because long-term supply contracts for dairy products are uncommon, they are usually obtained on the open market. As a result, ice cream manufacturers have limited price stability and frequently employ strategies like hedging to mitigate the effects of price fluctuation. Large ice cream manufacturers usually have a lot of negotiating leverage, but the lack of long-term contracts makes switching suppliers cheap. Large dairies and companies that offer bulk milk fat and concentrates to the ice cream industry, as well as companies that supply branded components, all contribute to increase supplier control to some extent. Leading manufacturers must maintain product quality to maintain brand equity. As a result, manufacturers will need to source high-quality raw materials.

Suppliers of technical equipment and industrial equipment for the production and processing of products in stock. Choosing the right supplier and equipment can either increase or decrease productivity.

In this industry, packaging is a crucial input, and some participants may enter into long-term contracts with suppliers. Some minor ingredients can be substituted, but key elements like milk and sugar have no adequate substitutes. This strengthens the supplier's position. Supplier power is evaluated as average overall.
Power of new entrants
Power of new entrants
Source: MarketLine research extended by author

Despite the ice cream market's maturity, individual gourmet ice cream makers will appeal to customers by emphasizing their use of natural ingredients and overall product quality. Specialty ice creams can be sold for a higher price, and any early investment in raw materials and manufacturing equipment can be recouped by the final product's high profit margins.
Larger firms are also involved in the production of mass-market items. Here, the margins are substantially smaller.

Distribution networks are another impediment to entry in this sector. Fresh ice cream is inherently difficult to transport, and storage costs are extremely expensive. Producers must make their products available to a vast number of supermarkets, each of which has significant purchasing power. It might be difficult to persuade merchants to devote sales room to a new player's product because it is a valued but scarce resource.

International firms, such as Unilever, operate their own ice cream parlor operations that sell to consumers directly. Smaller firms, on the other hand, may not be able to afford such outlets and must instead rely on supermarkets, specialized stores, and other retailers to stock their goods.

Manufacturers of ice cream can differentiate their products. According to consumers, ice cream should be associated with comfort and flavor discovery. Market differentiation makes it more difficult for newcomers to woo clients away from established businesses and limits the number of new entries.

Because switching costs are modest, retailers can simply modify their preferences. Retailers, on the other hand, must keep things in stock that are in high demand. The market grew rapidly throughout the period under study, increasing the likelihood of new entrants. The risk of new entrants is moderate in general.
Threat of substitutes
Threat of substitutes
Source: MarketLine research extended by author

Consumers can choose from a range of commercially available ice cream substitutes. Among them are frozen cakes, sorbets, confectionary, savory nibbles, and smoothies. Retailers should keep a choice of options on hand. It can provide advantages to the maker, such as larger profits, fewer storage costs, and extended shelf-life.

The expense of switching is negligible. Most stores, on the other hand, will almost certainly continue to sell ice cream as part of their product line. Substitutes are regarded as posing a moderate risk.
Rivalry
Power of rivalry
Source: MarketLine research extended by author

The Russian ice cream market is highly fragmented, with the top four companies accounting for 38.6 percent of the total market volume. Among its most important participants are large, well-known foreign corporations like Unilever and Froneri International Limited, as well as local players as Talosto Shahty and the Renna Group of Companies. Increased rivalry has resulted from the rise of strong operating firms as well as a high number of new entries into this industry. Retailers can quickly choose between goods from different suppliers, enhancing competition.

It's worth noting that the market's biggest companies possess a lot of manufacturing plants. As a result, exit barriers are high, because getting out of the market requires selling these specialized properties. Although fixed costs are anticipated to remain high, automated methods allow output to be scaled up as needed. Rivalry appears to be exacerbated by these factors.

Some of the industry's heavyweights have expanded into other food-related businesses, reducing competition by reducing their reliance on ice cream sales. Over the analysis period, strong demand growth tends to reduce competition. This industry has a modest level of competitiveness overall.
Summary
Porter 5 forces analysis
The decomposition of the criteria used to determine the influence of each component on the industry is explained in the following analysis.

Larger enterprises appear to be more diversified, reducing part of the load, whereas the ice cream sector is characterized by high operational and exit costs. Strong development over the assessment period will reduce rivalry (2015-2019).

Convenience stores, hypermarkets, and supermarkets are the primary buyers. Consumer purchasing decisions are impacted by branding, and retailers must match consumer demand, thus their purchasing power is limited.

Despite the fact that there are few acceptable alternatives for major commodities, producers can obtain their inputs from a comparably wide selection of providers. Provider of technical equipment is vital in terms of production capability and inner warehouse logistics.
Although there are ice cream substitutes, stores are likely to continue to sell ice cream as part of their product line. As a result, the risk of substitution is considered low.
New entrants face a significant barrier to entry due to well-established firms, yet it is becoming increasingly easier to break into the market on a smaller scale with a more artisan product.

These findings allow us to understand that the products will be in demand by the consumer. Unilever Rus is a well-established manufacturer of frozen desserts and a croissant stakeholder in the frozen desserts market. Despite this, the ice cream industry involves high costs for production      and internal logistics.

sub_elements.title: 1.3. Organization of warehouse logistics of the company
text: The company has its own central distribution warehouses, where products are stored and sorted for subsequent shipment to destination regions, as well as its own regional distribution centers (distribution warehouses). Also in the immediate vicinity of the factories are small warehouses for short-term storage of the company's finished products.

Some of the company's customers have sufficient storage capacity to place orders in their own warehouses. In this case, the products are delivered to the customer's warehouse directly from the Unilever factory.

For storage and distribution, the company also uses its own warehouses with the help of 3-PL providers. This, in turn, is due to the large volumes of products sold, which makes it expedient to maintain their own storage facilities. In addition, the company actively uses cross-docking operations, which involve the delivery of products to an intermediate point, where goods are reloaded into smaller vehicles and delivered to final retail outlets, thus bypassing the need for storage and sorting in a distribution warehouse.

This thesis is focused on the robotization of intra-warehouse processes of Unilever's own regional ice cream distribution warehouse located in the city of Tula. The low-temperature warehouse of Unilever Rus LLC in Tula is a modern Class A complex with a total capacity of more than 20,000 pallet spaces, equipped with high-tech equipment. Below is a spatial model of the warehouse (Fig. 10).
layout of Unilever’s warehouse in Tula

The warehouse logistics of the regional ice cream distribution warehouse includes the functions of storing, distributing, and sorting goods for their subsequent distribution to retail outlets of the company's customers. The specificity of warehouse processes is the need to constantly maintain a special temperature regime in storage areas (-24 degrees Celsius), as well as the high speed of warehouse operations performed outside the refrigeration zones.
Warehouse business processes

Warehouse business processes are organized as follows: products are delivered to the warehouse mainly from the ice cream factory located in Tula, a number of product items are delivered from the nearest warehouses. Upon arrival, the products are loaded into the expedition area, where they go through the acceptance procedure, which includes incoming control of the quality and quantity of goods, checking the accompanying documentation, loading the necessary information about the products into the WMS system, and notifying the WMS system of receipt. At the end of the acceptance procedure, the goods are moved by a forklift to the buffer zone or forwarding zone, where they undergo the process of sorting and marking and then await assignment and placement in the storage area. Storekeepers and drivers of forklifts and electric stackers receive all the necessary information about the current location, as well as the destination of each product, using the WMS system, into which the data is loaded by the employee responsible for placement (storekeeper, shift supervisor, warehouse manager, and others). After receiving the task, the forklift driver takes the necessary goods from the buffer zone and places them in the designated cells of the storage zone. Further, as orders are received in the picking area, goods are picked based on the WMS box picking list. Upon completion of the picking, the products are moved to the forwarding area, pass the final control and leave the warehouse. Below is a graphical representation of the sequence of warehouse business processes. It should be noted that floor industrial transport is used in most business processes in the warehouse. According to internal data, this is more than 70% of the time from the moment the goods arrive at the warehouse until loading.

sub_elements.title: 1.4. Problems and directions for improving the organization of warehouse logistics
text: In the course of a systematic analysis of warehouse work, taking into account the turbulent external economic situation in Russia, several problems were identified and directions for their timely solution were identified.

From Porter's analysis above, we can see that the ice cream industry is dependent on the strength of suppliers who supply food, equipment and other technical equipment. In the favorable  scenario, a modern, high-capacity industrial truck can improve productivity. In an unfavorable scenario, industrial vehicles can slow down workflows due to low capacity and productivity. This is the first and systemic problem.

Due to political events and the volatility of the ruble, the company indexed salaries, which significantly increased the company's expenses. According to the company's employees, the increase was about 55%. As logistics supply chains were disrupted, this also had a financial impact on product costs and intra-warehouse logistics costs. This is the second problem caused by the external turbulent environment.

Background: From the analysis of the industry, it was determined that the strength of suppliers, namely the choice of technical equipment, will affect the productive storage capacity of the warehouse. Increasing the salaries of employees as part of the improvement of living standards and indexation significantly increased the company's costs. It should be noted that floor industrial transport is used in most business processes in the warehouse. According to internal data, this is more than 70% of the time from the moment the goods arrive at the warehouse until loading.
Problem 1: High costs for warehouse assistance personnel, taking into account the increase in the standard of living of employees:

For 2022, the company has increased employee salaries by 55%. This significantly affected the company's costs and warehouse logistics as well. It is also worth noting that many operations that are performed by manual labor can be replaced by robotic ones, thereby reducing overall personnel costs and replacing them with machines.
Solution to consider:

It is necessary to consider possible alternatives to replacing manual labor with machine labor. it is possible that an increase in productivity on the part of the worker can positively influence the overall productivity relative to the required business processes in the warehouse.

Background: Since more than 2/3 of the time from the moment warehouse operations are carried out using floor industrial transport, it makes sense to consider options for increasing productivity and reducing time, and therefore man-costs for warehouse operations
Problem 2: The lack of productivity of floor industrial transport relative to the possibilities at the technological level.

The company is facing the need to increase productivity in the warehouse, and the company's strategy for 2025 should use more robotic vehicles. Employees of the company recognize that there are more technologically advanced warehouse vehicles on the market that will increase productivity. It is also worth noting that due to the complex logistics in the world, the purchase of foreign equipment is difficult.
Solution to consider:

To study the possibility of purchasing and using industrial floor transport with a large volume of productivity. This will require additional costs, as well as calculations of equipment payback. In particular, it is proposed to use loaders with side grips.

sub_elements.title: Chapter 1 Conclusions
text: Thus, Unilever is one of the absolute leaders in the industry of food and household chemicals. The company uses its advantage to increase the efficiency of its activities. In particular, the company has a significant amount of its own warehouse capacity and devotes sufficient resources and attention to the coordination and management of warehouses for various purposes, despite the independence of the unit due to the special operation in Ukraine. So, already at the moment there are a number of methods and principles of warehouse logistics management, implemented in the daily work of warehouse facilities. Principles warehouse management, as well as the technical equipment of the warehouse, allow achieving a high level of service. The warehouse management of the company is advanced for the region of presence, however, with the high efficiency of warehouse processes, two main directions were identified for a possible increase in the efficiency of the company's warehouse facilities.
Thus, in order to solve the existing deficiencies in management, it is necessary to consider ways to increase productivity using advanced industrial transport.

For theoretical substantiation and implementation of these recommendations, a detailed analysis of advanced industrial floor transport, a calculation of payback and an analysis of possible risks is required

text: For the robotization of warehouse business processes, several zones have been identified that will be subject to modifications. For this, the author took the analysis of the value chain as a basis. In this chapter, we will review logistics business processes by focusing on outbound logistics. We will also highlight areas that can be optimized by introducing robots and optimizing existing industrial floor transport, as well as consider technical solutions on the market for refrigerated warehouses.

sub_elements.title: 2.1. Value chain of Unilever Rus
text: The value chain concept is based on the organizational process, which is the idea of viewing a manufacturing (or service) organization as a system made up of subsystems, each with inputs, transformation processes, and outputs. The acquisition and consumption of resources such as money, labor, materials, equipment, buildings, land, administration, and management are all part of the inputs, transformation processes, and outputs. The manner in which value chain activities are carried out determines costs and has an impact on profits.
Porters generic value chain
Primary activities
Inbound transportation

One of the most crucial components is strong connections with suppliers, since their assistance is required for collecting, storing, and delivering the goods. A lack of study of Unilever internal logistics might lead to issues throughout the product development process. The process necessitates the company's focus on every phase of the shift from raw materials to completed goods. The manufacturing of raw resources, storage of resources, and internal distribution of components and materials for the start of production are all examples of inbound logistics.
Operations

Once the raw materials are obtained, it is necessary to process them and turn them into final products. In this regard, the importance of operational activities increases. Operating example: machining, packaging, assembly and testing. Repair and maintenance of equipment is also part of the operating activities.
At this stage, in addition to the production of products, the repair of technical equipment takes place. This includes manufacturing and service operations.
Outbound logistics

Outbound logistics is all about delivering products to customers or end users — orders going out. Outbound logistics include actions that transport a product to a client via various intermediaries. Material handling, warehousing, scheduling, order processing, transportation, and delivering to the destination are examples of outbound logistics operations. Unilever can analyze and optimize its outbound logistics in order to identify sources of competitive advantage and meet its business growth objectives.

The firm specifies its standards for departing logistics in order to maintain the right state of goods. The contract with the logistics service provider, among other things, specified the conditions for product transportation. These standards state that product transportation (loading / unloading) shall not degrade product quality or pose a danger to product safety.

It is also worth noting that warehouse operations are outsourced. On February 1, 2016, TLS successfully launched the Project for the provision of safekeeping and warehouse management services at the low-temperature warehouse of Unilever Rus LLC in Tula.

Additional criteria govern the credentials of the logistics service provider's service staff, as well as the responsibility for teaching safety standards, the use of personal protective equipment, accident reporting, and emergency response protocols. It is important to note that the ice-cream industry is quite seasonal, which is why the need for logistical services is much higher during peak season (May-September).
Marketing and Sales

At this stage, Unilever will emphasize the benefits and differentiators of the supplied items in order to persuade customers that its offering is superior than rivals. Unilever cannot create value by creating a high-quality product at a reasonable price with distinguishing characteristics unless it engages in marketing and sales operations. Salespeople and marketers are crucial in this situation.

Unilever's marketing and sales efforts include sales force, advertising, promotional activities, pricing, channel selection, quoting, and relationship development with channel members. The marketing funnel strategy may be used to structure the company's marketing and sales operations. Depending on Unilever's corporate objectives, brand image, competitive dynamics, and existing market position, marketing tactics can be push or pull in nature.
However, it is important to note that Unilever Rus has now stopped all media and advertising spending.
Service

Unilever attempts in every manner feasible with an actual and future client to help them. There is always a live feedback line on the site. Buyer will always be able to obtain an answer to his inquiry. The firm leads the social media network for each one of his brands, attempting to generate activity and provide the chance to become familiar with their goods.
Support activities
Firm infrastructure

The company is involved in a variety of infrastructure tasks, including quality management, legal challenges, accounting, finance, planning, and strategic management. Unilever can optimize the value of the whole value chain with effective infrastructure management. The company provides yearly sustainability reports that detail accomplishments and progress toward targets.
Human resources
The firm makes an effort to acquire outside talent (for example, students, experienced workers).

Recruitment occurs and is automated by artificial intelligence, as well as in person face to face. Through DEGREED, a corporate learning platform, the corporation is attempting to invest in staff training. It should also be emphasized that the corporation is heavily reliant on its employees when analyzing risks, it is important to consider (talents).
Technological development

Almost the whole chain is now reliant on technology assistance. Unilever must execute the necessity of technological development through technological integration in production, distribution, marketing, and human operations. Some examples include using technology to automate software, customer support, product creation, and data analysis. This category includes Unilever's R&D department. When new technologies are introduced, the firm employs simulation models to visualize production. Such technologies will have a modest impact on business.
Procurement

Procurement (purchasing) in the value chain refers to the activities involved with acquisition, which can range from the purchase of equipment, machinery, and other goods required for the manufacturing of the completed product. Unilever, through its affiliation with numerous networks, must carefully analyze its procurement activities to optimize the entering, operating, and departing value chain. Because the system is currently opaque, it is required to enhance the optimization of incoming purchases to prevent wasting resources. It is also critical to focus on increasing the productivity of industrial vehicles, since this might affect labor productivity.
Value chain outcomes

Based on the analysis of the value chain, it is worth noting that the business processes taking place in the ice cream warehouse in the city of Tula relate to outbound logistics and include 11 stages from receiving goods to sending them to the retailer.

From the value chain analysis, we found that Unilever's outbound logistics need to be reviewed more frequently and have the potential to be optimized to achieve a competitive advantage in the market. Moreover, the IS warehouse in the city of Tula is outsourced by the Russian company TLS, which makes its own adjustments to the work of the warehouse. Also, based on supporting activities, namely procurement, it turned out that the procurement system is not transparent due to the geographical scope and asymmetry of information in equipment procurement. In this regard, it is important to increase the optimization of technical equipment and labor productivity.
2.2. Defining zones for robotization

In this part, we will look at warehouse business processes and place them within the value chain analysis. In this way, we will be able to understand the weak areas in the chain of operations and outbound logistics.
Part of operation processes
Receipt of goods
At this stage, goods are accepted from the factory or goods from safekeeping warehouses from other cities.
Unloading

Unloading of ice cream at a safekeeping warehouse in the city of Tula is carried out using dock shelters. This is done so that warm air from the street does not enter the car body and into the storage room. Products are unloaded immediately inside the warehouse - to the expedition (acceptance) zone. Warehouse employees try to unload and release the car as quickly as possible. Abroad, for the transportation of deep-frozen products, refrigerators with the so-called sliding floor are often used. Using such devices, the driver can independently unload up to 30 pallets into the expedition area. The duration of this process does not exceed several minutes.
Acceptance

Immediately after the goods are unloaded into the expedition area, they are identified, accepted (entered into the WMS database) and sent to the main storage area, where the temperature regime is maintained at -18 °C and below.
Difficulties in accepting deep-frozen products may arise for the following reasons:
lack of necessary supporting documentation;
non-compliance with the temperature regime of transportation;
the inability to read the barcode printed on the package with a scanner;
violation of the integrity of the packaging, discrepancy between the goods received after the fact and the TTN, etc.
Sorting
Further, the goods are sorted according to the principle of a pallet and a box zone, intended for large retailers and for small enterprises.
Label sticking

Next comes the labeling of goods for the Mercury system. FSIS "Mercury" (Federal State Information System "Mercury") is an automated system for electronic certification of cargoes, which are subject to state veterinary control in the territory of the Russian Federation.
Allocation

With a significant cargo turnover, it is advisable to trust WMS to determine the location of a particular commodity item in a warehouse. If we are talking about the storage of deep-frozen products, it is necessary to take into account the turnover of the product and its expiration date. Goods with a higher turnover and a shorter shelf life, the system tries to place as close as possible to the forwarding zone. WMS determines the turnover of goods independently, the expiration date is indicated on the label of the goods and is determined by the system automatically when reading the barcode or entered manually by warehouse employees.
At the moment, the work on the placement of pallets is carried out by personnel using industrial floor transport.
Goods storage
The distribution of the cell and tier on the drive-in pallet racking is based on the ABC analysis, which is determined by the WMS system
Part of outbound logistics processes
Picking process

The drive-in pallet rack has the function of picking up a pallet in a certain cell along the conveyor. However, after that, the pallet is placed in the consolidation area and removed after the end of the goods on the pallet. The fence of the desired article is carried out manually with the help of floor industrial transport under the guidance of loaders. It also uses slack, which implies a lack of system control and increases the risk of human error. In this regard, the picking time increases and there is a risk of damage to the package.
Output control

Collected orders are placed in the expedition area (air temperature -24 degrees), where they are checked and immediately after the check they are shipped to the refrigerator - the temperature in the body must correspond to the temperature in the main storage area. Before the start of the morning shopping, the products are stored in the back of a refrigerated truck connected to the warehouse's power supply system.
Package

Further, the collected orders are scorched. The procedure is performed with special equipment - a palletizer. After that, the singed products are moved next to the docking stations for long-distance shipment.
Shipment

When working in cold rooms, forklift operators have to work in warm clothes, hats, thick shoes. This makes it difficult to control the machine, makes drivers less sensitive to the behavior of equipment, and increases their fatigue. Also in the summer, temperature fluctuations negatively affect a person. It is worth paying attention to the speed of moving pallets from the consolidation zone to the car body, since the temperature difference (in summer) will adversely affect the state of frozen desserts. It is very important to unload as soon as possible in compliance with temperature conditions.
After analyzing the value chain and business processes from the operations stage and proceeding from the logistics, the following weak points can be identified.

This implies a lack of control over the system and increases the risk of human error. It is necessary to automate this process, taking into account the correct data entered into the WMS system.

Based on the data from the above table, we see that the theses from the value chain analysis meet with problems in the field of business processes in the safekeeping warehouse in the city of Tula. In this regard, it can be concluded that:

The unloading zone needs robotization. Goods entering the warehouse should be received in a faster time with the help of modern equipment, which is introduced in overseas warehouses, but is still not used by the company.
The acceptance area should improve the scanning process (make it so that the articles are read without re-reading attempts) and reduce the time it takes to import data into the WMS system.
The allocation process needs to be improved by automation. We need industrial floor transport, which will help to deliver pallets after marking to the storage area.

In the picking area, it is necessary to improve the order collection system with automatic data entry into the warehouse system. This will help improve assembly accuracy and reduce order assembly time for retailers.
In the dispatch area, it is necessary to reduce the time of loading pallets into the machine, taking into account the preservation of the temperature regime and with the minimum use of human labor.

sub_elements.title: 2.3. Technical solution for each optimization zone
text: In this part, we will discuss the technical solutions for each optimization area using robotization and automation of industrial floor vehicles.
STACKER ROBOT

To automate the acceptance area, allocation, picking and departure process, it was proposed to introduce a robot. Self-driving forklift with adaptive behavior technologies for efficient pallet handling in warehouses with heavy people and vehicle traffic.
ROBOCV stacker robot

The robot is equipped with algorithms for intelligent interaction with other vehicles: maneuvering, slowing down, stopping
When moving, the robot uses turn signals to indicate maneuvers.
All roads are prioritized.
When entering a road with a higher priority, the robot lets the vehicles moving along it pass, and only then passes itself.
Each robot road can be configured individually: right-hand, left-hand, center-traffic, and one-way roads are available.
The robot continuously monitors the direction and speed of vehicles around itself and, in the event of a possible intersection of trajectories, reduces the speed.
Low productivity

Having skipped the technique, the robot continues to work. The robot slows down in front of the "blind" zones. When meeting with other vehicles in a narrow alley, the robot can cuddle up to the side of the road, freeing up the passage
Low productivity when the warehouse is overcrowded
Work with conveyors and racks.
Robots are able to take and put pallets on conveyors and different tiers of racks
Judging by the dimensions of the equipment, work with cells no higher than the third tier
Pallet control during transportation.
When transporting a pallet, the robot continuously monitors its position on the forks with sensors
Working with pallet cargo.
Robots are able to automatically detect the presence of pallets in the buffer cells in motion and immediately drive up to them, without wasting time on empty cells.
Robots are able to capture and transport pallets of various sizes (EUR, FIN, as well as other standard and non-standard pallets) and materials: wood, plastic, metal.
When picking up a pallet, the robot determines its position and builds an accurate trajectory, which allows it to pick up pallets that are slightly offset or at an angle.

Examples of situations in which the robot will not pick up a pallet: the side leg of the pallet is missing; a strong deviation of the dimensions of the pallet from the given ones; a significant shift or rotation of the pallet;
pallet quality
pallets must always be level
Possible robotic processes:
1. Transportation of incoming pallets (acceptance)
the robot determines the position of the pallet and scans the barcode
the robot transports the pallet to the conveyor;
2. Transportation of outgoing pallets (shipment)
the robot determines the position of the pallet and scans the barcode
the robot transports the pallet from the conveyor to the dock;
3. Cross-docking (between buffers and zones)
the robot determines the position of the pallet and scans the barcode
the robot transports the pallet from the buffer zone to the dock;
4. Order selection (repalletization/repacking)
the robot brings pallets with goods to the point of selection
the picker is at the pickup point
robots provide continuous delivery of pallets to the assembler
LOADER WITH SIDE ARMS
The multi-way sideloader (sideloader, sidelift) is equipped with a special transport platform that allows you to conveniently move long and bulky loads in confined spaces.
Loader with side arms
Main advantage and feature is that loading equipment with side grip is distinguished by such advantages as:
Versatility. The purchase of this equipment will allow you to perform several operations - to transport and stack long loads;
Significant load capacity. If you decide to buy a multi-way side loader, you will be able to load products weighing up to 50,000 kilograms from the side and move them to a height of up to 8 meters;
Ease of transportation. Due to the presence of a wide transport platform, the equipment quickly handles bulky goods;
Efficiency. The side loading forklift makes the most efficient use of available space, thereby reducing operating costs.
Possible potential for the introduction of this technique:
Involvement in loading fewer employees
Reducing loading / unloading time

We could implement this solution as a way to increase the productivity of industrial trucks, but there are reasons why we cannot do this. One of them is the requirements for the transportation of goods.
Below is an R&D message, which is why this solution cannot be implemented.
R&D comment:

The current scheme of transportation of Finished Goods does not involve transportation on the side surface of the box. The direction of the corrugations in the three-layer box is vertical for even distribution of the load of the upper boxes. Transporting on the side will result in jams and, consequently, damage to the GPU. For the use of such transportation, we see great risks, we consider it inappropriate to conduct tests for the current laying schemes.

Calculation of the required quantity of the proposed floor industrial transport, taking into account the load of the warehouse.

sub_elements.title: 3.2. Questions regarding corporate social responsibility
text: Given the successful launch of the above 2 robotic and automated solutions, the question will arise of what to do with the personnel whose labor has been replaced.

In my opinion, people, having freed themselves from the shackles of routine, will either retrain and leave for other professions, or remain in the same areas, but will be engaged in more complex and highly intellectual tasks.

It is important to note that warehouse operations are not performed by Unilever employees, but by an outsourced company. Accordingly, when replacing human labor with robotic, the cost of outsourcing warehouse services should decrease.

Despite the fact that the employees of the company will not have to be fired, the question of reducing the employees of the partner company will arise. Despite this, the question of the need for personnel who can train workers to work with robotic industrial floor vehicles and repair automated solutions will be relevant.

sub_elements.title: Chapter 3 conclusion
text: In this chapter, questions were considered regarding the calculation of the required number of robotic and automated floor industrial transport. The data for the calculations were provided by a 3pl company that provides warehouse services for Unilever Rus in the city of Tula.

Calculations were carried out as part of the as is/to be analysis. For the calculation regarding the stacker robot, current data and indicators for 2021 were taken. It should be noted that the figures are averaged, respectively, during the period of high and low seasonality, the data may vary. Calculations were considered for each group of stakeholders at each stage of business processes, where the use of a stacker robot is recommended.
Also in this chapter, the ethical question was raised about what to do with workers whose work will be replaced by robotic and automated solutions.

table_of_content.title: Conclusion
text: As part of this final qualification work, value chains and business processes were analyzed, as well as ways to improve warehouse logistics. Over the past year, the company has increased the salaries of employees by 55%, which entailed additional costs. Moreover, the systemic problem of improving industrial floor transport at the IC warehouse in the city of Tula entails a lack of development in the speed and productivity of pallet handling. The issue of introducing robotic and automated solutions is becoming increasingly relevant, therefore, in order not to lose competitiveness, enterprises must implement tools that will allow them to get higher productivity.
To achieve the set goal, which is to offer robotic and automated solutions for the supply chain of Unilever Russia Ice Cream Department, a consistent solution of the following tasks was implemented:
Analytical review of the situation in the warehouse;
Industry analysis of the ice cream industry in Russia;
Analysis of the current performance of Unilever Rus in the field of sustainable development through the analysis of the value chain;
Determination the points in the business processes of the warehouse using the analysis of the value chain and business processes;
Offering technical problem solving capabilities;
Economic evaluation of recommendations.

Chapter 1 was devoted to industry analysis and internal business processes, which helped to understand the trends and position of the company in the market. The analysis showed that the strength of suppliers is very important. Suppliers of technical equipment and their products influence the production capacity and intra-warehouse logistics. Also, 2 main problems were identified, one of which is systemic, and the other is caused by external factors:
High costs for warehouse assistance personnel, taking into account the increase in the standard of living of employees
The lack of productivity of floor industrial transport relative to the possibilities at the technological level.

In the second chapter, a value chain analysis was carried out. This tool helped to highlight the main directions of the company, which were later compared with the results of the current warehouse business processes. Thus, the overlay of these analyzes helped to highlight the weak areas in the ice cream warehouse in Tula. These are areas where inconsistencies were identified from the analysis of the value chain and the real picture in the warehouse:
unloading
acceptance
allocation
picking
Automated and robotic solutions were proposed for the respective areas in view of the stacker and loader robot with long forks and the relevance of their application was substantiated.

In the third chapter, the results of calculations regarding the required number of purchased and rented industrial vehicles were presented, and the ethical issue regarding t = retraining of personnel replaced by a robot was mentioned.
