Questions regarding corporate social responsibility 55 Chapter 3 conclusion 56 Conclusion 57 References 59 Attachment 62 Introduction Currently, logistics management is one of the important factors for the company's value chain. Proper supply chain management makes it possible not only to quickly deliver products from point A to point B, but also makes it possible to optimize costs even in a turbulent external environment.  Warehousing services are one of the most important elements of the supply chain. They allow not only to implement the storage of the necessary stocks formed in the production process and distribution, but rationally manage the outgoing flow throughout the supply chain.   Effective management of warehouse business processes using automati zati on and robotization can significantly reduce company costs and achieve competitive advantage due to the high efficiency of storage  facilities.   “Automation” is the process or act of turning the control of a device or machine into a more automatic system. Generally, “automatization” has the same meaning.  Robotization is the displacement of people from the productive process, with their replacement by automated and robotic machines and production lines, in connection with which resources are released for the development of the service sector. The chosen format of the final qualification work is a consulting project for Unilever.  The relevance  of the topic is explained by the increase in the wages of employees by 55% due to the company's policy and the functional problem of the low productivity of industrial transport. For 2022, the company increased the salaries of employees by 55%, which significantly affected both the company's costs and warehouse logistics. Unilever is facing the need to increase productivity in the warehouse and the company's strategy for 2025 is to use more robotic vehicles, even company employees admit that there are more technologically advanced warehouse vehicles on the market that will increase productivity. The managerial problem  is the need to introduce robotic and automated solutions in the supply chain at the Unilever Rus safe storage warehouse (ice cream product). The main goal  of the thesis is to   p ropose robotic and automated solutions, determining weak zones of warehouse business process to increase capability of floor industrial transport  for Unilever Rus ice cream division. To achieve this goal, it is necessary sequential solution of the following tasks: Analytical review of the situation in the warehouse; I ndustry analysis of the ice cream industry in  Russia ; Analy sis of  the current performance of Unilever Rus in the field of sustainable development through the analysis of the value  chain ; Determ ination  the points in the business processes of the warehouse using the analysis of the value chain and business  processes ; Offer ing  technical problem solving  capabilities ; Economic evaluation of recommendations. The  object of the  thesis  is the Unilever Rus company  ( IC division ) , the  subject is robotization and automation of logistics.  The work consists of three chapters: the first is devoted to the analysis of the current activities of the company, as well as the analysis of the microenvironment. For their implementation, the author will use Porter's 5 Forces frameworks. The second chapter is devoted to the analysis of the value chain and warehouse business processes to identify weak areas in the process of operations and outbound logistics. Relevant technological solutions will be proposed in accordance with the conclusions. The third chapter is devoted to recommendations and a miscalculation from payback. The article deals with both primary and secondary data sources. Primary data refer to interviews with company representatives. Secondary data includes documents and books related to the subject of research (MarketLine, Springer) and other data. The proposed toolkit, which allows solving the problem : value chain concept by Michal Porter ; Porter's 5 Five analysis ; Analytical formulas for calculating the need for personnel; Analytical formulas for calculating the need for technological equipment . CHAPTER 1. COMPANY AND INDUSTRY ANALYSIS This chapter examines the activities of Unilever Rus. as well as its current performance. This will also include a market overview, a macro-environmental study, and a competitive analysis. The following tools will be used in the research: Porter's Five Forces analysis. Porter's Five Forces model aids in analyzing industry rivalry and factors that influence its attractiveness.   1.1. Brief history, current state of the company and its structure Unilever entered the Russian market in 1991, with the opening of the first office in Moscow in 1992, representing " UniRus ." They began by importing only the Lux,  Omo , Signal, Impulse, and Lipton brands. In 1994, Unilever established its Russian headquarters in St. Petersburg, and by July 1995, the company had acquired its first plant in Russia,  Severnoye   Siyaniye . Unilever, on the other hand, initially concentrated on chemical products and established the Russian office of Van den Bergen Jurgens B.V. in 1996 to expand into the food and beverage sector. Rama,  Delmi , Calve, and Brook Bond were introduced to the Russian market during this period. However, they were all imported decades ago. Calve goods originally arrived in Russia in 1996, but were imported from the Netherlands and distributed by  UniRus  until 1998. By 1998, these entirely imported commodities had increased to 7% of the market. (Unilever, 2008) Imports were halted in the aftermath of the 1998 financial crisis. Calve mayonnaise manufacture was developed in Russia in 1999. The brand's market share has continuously increased since 2001, when it entered Ukraine; the same year, Calve ketchup was produced; and in 2003, Calve sauces were introduced (History of Brenda Calve 2005).  The corporation's operations in Russia have continued to grow. While Unilever built its own facilities,  the majority of  its expansion was accomplished through acquisitions of local players. As a result, Unilever remained true to its headquarters development strategy while mitigating the risks associated with Greenfield initiatives in industrialized countries. Unilever agreed in 2009 to acquire the sauce business of a Russian company,  Baltimor .  Baltimor  is Russia's largest manufacturer of ketchup.  Baltimor  began operations in late 1990" in Saint Petersburg (and was first meant to serve only this region), but quickly recognized the product's  promise was far broader and began spreading throughout Russia.  Baltimor  entered the market ahead of big multinational competitors, utilizing an aggressive marketing strategy and establishing connections with distributors to take more than half of the Russian ketchup market by the year 2000. The business began to grow when it acquired an agricultural estate in southern Russia in 2004 and began producing canned veggies. Despite the fact that the new business was extremely profitable, accounting for 25% of  Baltimor's  earnings from 2005 to 2007,  Baltimor  has invested extensively in business growth, resulting in the company's net debt reaching 90 million dollars. Simultaneously, the founders of the company agreed to create their own butter factory in Krasnodar. The estimated cost of the factory was $12 million; the actual cost was $25 million. The firm was compelled to implement cost-cutting measures  as a result of  difficulty refinancing debt. One of these milestones was the conclusion of the arrangement with the 3PL, with whom the ketchup division was affiliated. None of the initiatives, however, were sufficiently bold, and Unilever acquired  Baltimor's  most valuable and successful company, the ketchup group, in 2009.  The company's worldwide management consistently emphasizes the critical nature of the Russian division's and the Russian economy's development. This strategy contributes to the company's overall strength. Unilever was placed 34th in Forbes magazine's 2015 ranking of the "50 largest foreign firms in Russia," and 27th in 2016.  At the moment , the company's brand portfolio in Russia may be classified conditionally into four divisions, each of which is further subdivided into global and local brands, as seen in Table 1. Unilever’ company products Global Local Cosmetic products Axe, Dove, Lux, Rexona,  Sunsilk ,  Clear vita ABE,  Timotei «Чистая линия», «Черный жемчуг», «Калина», «Бархатные ручки», «Лесной бальзам», «Сто рецептов красоты» Household chemicals Omo , Surf,  Domestos ,  Glorix ,  Cif – Food products Hellmann’s, Knorr, Rama, Calve,  Crème Bonjour « Балтимор », « Восточный   гурман », « Пышка » Drinks and ice cream Инмарко , Lipton, Magnum,  Brooke Bond «Торжество», «Экзо», «Беседа», «Сан- Кремо » This breadth of brands necessitates that the company's management pay special attention to the organizational structure. To ensure that the business operates well in today's market conditions and is capable of achieving its objectives, the management frequently revises the organizational structure of departments and the relationships between them.  All of  these adjustments at Unilever Russia are aimed at ensuring that the company can operate in the following seven functional areas invariably:  1.the marketing function is responsible for the company's brand development and product range  formation;   2.the supply chain management function is responsible for the planning, procurement, production, transportation, warehousing, and quality assurance of finished  products;   3.the sales function is responsible for ensuring that products are available on store shelves throughout Russia, Belarus, and Ukraine by establishing and servicing a customer base;  4.the financial function is responsible for the company's financial health.  5. the function of information technology, contributing to the introduction of the latest management systems and providing technical support for all other  functions; 6. the personnel management function, which is responsible for recruiting and developing employees, paying compensation, resolving administrative issues, and resolving internal communication problems;  7. the corporate communications function, which is responsible for interacting with government agencies and the media. Structure of Unilever Historically, the company has developed a corporate structure. This was the impetus for the development of the independence branch, which has survived to this day: the power and management of the company are divided geographically. Strategic management is carried out mainly by the executive director, as well as directors,  geographic and functional purpose.  There are five executive positions: Director of Finance, Director of Research and Development, Director of Human Resources, Director of Supply Chain, and Director of Marketing and Communications. Product division of management is carried out as follows: four directors are  responsible for four main areas (care products, household chemicals and other home care products, food and refreshing products). Group management is also divided into three main geographical segments: Americas, Western Europe, as well as a combination of segments for Africa, Asia, Central and Eastern Europe. The chief president of each segment is the regional and its Board of Registration.  The  above   disadvantages   include   management   features  ( Fig . 1). Structure of strategic management       For further management of divisions, each segment is subdivided into smaller structures: Markets of Russia,  Ukraine  and Belarus (Unilever RUB). Regional management structure The Russian division is united into a single organizational matrix structure. The division is carried out according to the main functional areas (Customer Relations Development Department, Production Planning and Logistics Department, Marketing Department) and is associated with support departments (Finance Department, Human Resources Department, Information and Technology Department, Public Relations Department). The management structure is shown in the figure below (Fig.  2 ). The management structure of the Russian division of Unilever However, due to recent events related to the military operation on the territory of Ukraine, this structure has undergone multiple changes.  At the moment , according to employees, Unilever Russia does not receive support from a global company and acts as an independent organization.  D ue to the sanctions, Unilever will suspend the export of products to Russia.  According to Bloomberg , the company will also stop investing in advertising and further projects in Russia. On March 8, the British company Unilever, one of the world leaders in the production of consumer goods, will suspend the export of products to the Russian Federation, as well as its import from Russia after the military operation launched by Moscow in Ukraine. Bloomberg reported this on Tuesday, citing a company representative. The company will continue to supply vital food and hygiene products made in  Russia, but  will keep this issue under close scrutiny. Thus, today the company "Unilever Rus" is the Russian division of an international company that has production, storage, transport facilities around the world, the effective functioning of which largely depends on competent logistics management and, in particular, on the management and organization of the company's storage  facilities . 1. 2  Impact of turbulent environment on company’s business operations On March 8, the British company Unilever announced a halt to work in Russia due to a special operation in Ukraine. In early March, the European office of the company announced that it would suspend work in Russia. The Russian factories of the British company Unilever had to revise the range of products due to sanctions. "Due to reduced imports of raw materials and packaging materials, our business will face a number of operational challenges. Our customers may see some changes in our product range, while the quality of products will not change." - said the representatives of the company.  How exactly the enterprises will revise the assortment, the company did not specify. But they noted that factories in Yekaterinburg, Omsk, St.  Petersburg  and Tula will continue to produce goods. And these are personal care products, household goods and food products. Among which are the "Gold Standard";  Inmarko . Moreover, Unilever has defined a living wage as an income level that covers a family's basic needs and "helps them break the cycle of poverty." The company has already raised salaries for its employees and wants to raise wages for people outside of its workforce to promote economic inclusion. According to Ekaterina  Sokolova , Warehouse Coordinator in Tula, since the beginning of 2022, the company has increased the salaries of all employees by 55%. Wages should be enough to cover food, water, housing, education, healthcare, transportation and clothing, as well as emergency expenses, the company said. “Unilever’s plan shows what responsible action is needed from the private sector to make an impact in the fight against inequality and help build a world where everyone has the opportunity to thrive, not just survive,” said Gabriela Bucher, chief executive of Oxfam. Oxfam is an international association fighting poverty around the world. In addition to this, says Natalya  Utkina , chief technologist of the Union of Ice Cream Workers of Russia, the period of purchase for the most profitable season for ice cream workers just fell on the growth of the dollar and the disruption of supply chains . What is more, due to the depreciation of the ruble, 20% of Russian companies including Unilever Russia have planned indexation or salary increases for staff in the near future, according to a survey by the Research Center of the SuperJob.ru portal, received on Sunday (March 6, 2022) by Interfax. The results of the study show that two-thirds of employers (66%) have indexed or increased the wages of their employees over the past year. The survey showed that almost all employers (98%) did not delay their employees' wages for the last month. The survey was conducted on March 2-4,  2022  among 1,000 respondents representing personnel management in 174 settlements of the Russian Federation, in all federal districts.  From the above arguments, we see that the company's personnel and logistics costs have increased greatly due to the sanctions and political events. In view of this, in order to optimize intra-warehouse logistics, the company is aware of the possibility and necessity of optimizing industrial transport and increasing productivity. 1.2.1.  Analysis of the Ice cream Industry  in Russia This part is dedicated to the analysis of the Russian ice cream market industry, which will be done using  Porter's 5 Forces to analyze competitors.  To assess the risks of the ice cream industry in an external turbulent environment, the author proposed to carry out a Porter analysis to understand the risks. This framework will help to take into account the strengths and weaknesses of the industry and the influence of various external factors. The results of the analysis will help in the formation of possible directions for solving the problem. Porter's Five Forces Framework is a method for assessing industry rivalry and the factors that impact it. It was introduced by Michael Porter and then published in a Harvard Business Review article. Threat of new entrants, competitive rivalry, threat of substitutes, supplier bargaining power, and buyer negotiating power are all incorporated in the instrument.  The author of this report rated each aspect on a scale of 1 to 5 (lowest threat to the industry) (the highest threat). Furthermore, each force was assessed based on a variety of industry-relevant criteria. ANALYSIS OF THE FIVE FORCES  Ice cream companies will participate in a study of the ice cream industry. Important sellers will include supermarkets, hypermarkets, specialized merchants, and grocery stores, while key suppliers will be raw material providers (such as milk, butter, and emulsifier producers). Power  of   buyers Power of buyers Source:  MarketLine research extended by author The most important customers in the Russian ice cream sector are convenience stores, which account for 45.2 percent of total market value. Hypermarkets and supermarkets are the second-largest distribution channel, accounting for 29.0 percent of the market's overall volume. Food retailing in Russia is  fragmented , allowing players to sell to a diverse group of customers.  Ice cream manufacturers can distinguish their products based on brand, flavor, and calorie level. Large purchasers typically require a wide range of products for their customers, which reduces their purchasing power marginally. Buying power drivers are particularly weakened when people are devoted to specific brands. Retailers are unlikely to be affected by brand loyalty, and price sensitivity remains high. Reverse integration has been attempted by several retailers; for example, supermarkets have established private label products. Large worldwide ice cream companies, on the other hand, have reacted by selling ice cream directly to customers through franchised ice cream parlors.  When it comes to exports, Russia provides a significant portion of the supply to the United States. Russian ice cream companies raised their exports to the United States by 3.7 times in the first quarter of 2021. ( compared  to January-March 2020). The United States has overtaken China as the world's largest buyer of Russian ice cream in terms of dollar value.   Overall, the buyer power is assessed as moderate.  Power of suppliers Power of suppliers Source: MarketLine research extended by author Because long-term supply contracts for dairy products are uncommon, they are usually obtained on the open market. As a result, ice cream manufacturers have limited price stability and frequently employ strategies like hedging to mitigate the effects of price fluctuation. Large ice cream manufacturers usually have a lot of negotiating leverage, but the lack of long-term contracts makes switching suppliers cheap. Large dairies and companies that offer bulk milk fat and concentrates to the ice cream industry, as well as companies that supply branded components, all contribute to increase supplier control to some extent. Leading manufacturers must maintain product quality to maintain brand equity. As a result, manufacturers will need to source high-quality raw materials. Suppliers of technical equipment and industrial equipment for the production and processing of products in stock. Choosing the right supplier and equipment can either increase or decrease productivity. In this industry, packaging is a crucial input, and some participants may enter into long-term contracts with suppliers. Some minor ingredients can be substituted, but key elements like milk and sugar have no adequate substitutes. This strengthens the supplier's position.  Supplier power is evaluated as average overall. Power  of   new   entrants Power of new entrants Source: MarketLine research extended by author Despite the ice cream market's maturity, individual gourmet ice cream makers will appeal to customers by emphasizing their use of natural ingredients and overall product quality. Specialty ice creams can be sold for a higher price, and any early investment in raw materials and manufacturing equipment can be recouped by the final product's high profit margins.  Larger firms are also involved in the production of mass-market items. Here, the margins are substantially smaller.  Distribution networks are another impediment to entry in this sector. Fresh ice cream is inherently difficult to transport, and storage costs are extremely expensive. Producers must make their products available to a vast number of supermarkets, each of which has significant purchasing power. It might be difficult to persuade merchants to devote sales room to a new player's product because it is a valued but scarce resource.  International firms, such as Unilever, operate their own ice cream parlor operations that sell to consumers directly. Smaller firms, on the other hand, may not be able to afford such outlets and must instead rely on supermarkets, specialized stores, and other retailers to stock their goods. Manufacturers of ice cream can differentiate their products. According to consumers, ice cream should be associated with comfort and flavor discovery. Organization of warehouse logistics of the company The company has its own central distribution warehouses, where products are stored and sorted for subsequent shipment to destination regions, as well as its own regional distribution centers (distribution warehouses). Also in the immediate vicinity of the factories are small warehouses for short-term storage of the company's finished products. In addition, the company actively uses cross-docking operations, which involve the delivery of products to an intermediate point, where goods are reloaded into smaller vehicles and delivered to final retail outlets, thus bypassing the need for storage and sorting in a distribution warehouse. This thesis is focused on the robotization of intra-warehouse processes of Unilever's own regional ice cream distribution warehouse located in the city of Tula. The specificity of warehouse processes is the need to constantly maintain a special temperature regime in storage areas (-24 degrees Celsius), as well as the high speed of warehouse operations performed outside the refrigeration zones. Warehouse business processes Warehouse business processes are organized as follows: products are delivered to the warehouse mainly from the ice cream factory located in Tula,  a number of  product items are  delivered from the nearest warehouses. Upon arrival, the products are loaded into the expedition area, where they go through the acceptance procedure, which includes incoming control of the quality and quantity of goods, checking the accompanying documentation, loading the necessary information about the products into the WMS system, and notifying the WMS system of receipt. At the end of the acceptance procedure, the goods are moved by a forklift to the buffer zone or forwarding zone, where they undergo the process of sorting and marking and then await assignment and placement in the storage area. Storekeepers and drivers of forklifts and electric stackers receive all the necessary information about the current location, as well as the destination of each product, using the WMS system, into which the data is loaded by the employee responsible for placement (storekeeper, shift supervisor, warehouse manager, and others). After receiving the task, the forklift driver takes the necessary goods from the buffer zone and places them in the designated cells of the storage zone. Further, as orders are received in the picking area, goods are picked based on the WMS box picking list. Upon completion of the picking, the products are moved to the forwarding area, pass the final control and leave the warehouse. Value  chain   of  Unilever  Rus The value chain concept is based on the organizational process, which is the idea of viewing a manufacturing (or service) organization as a system made up of subsystems, each with inputs, transformation processes, and outputs. The  manner in which  value chain activities are carried out determines costs and has an impact on profits. Questions regarding corporate social responsibility Given the successful launch of the above 2 robotic and automated solutions, the question will arise of what to do with the personnel whose labor has been replaced. In my opinion, people, having freed themselves from the shackles of routine, will either retrain and leave for other professions, or remain in the same areas, but will be engaged in more complex and highly intellectual tasks. It is important to note that warehouse operations are not performed by Unilever employees, but by an outsourced company . Accordingly, when replacing human labor with robotic, the cost of outsourcing warehouse services should decrease. Despite the fact that  the employees of the company will not have to be fired, the question of reducing the employees of the partner company will arise.   Despite this, the question of the need for personnel who can train workers to work with robotic industrial floor vehicles and repair automated solutions will be relevant. Chapter 3 conclusion In this chapter, questions were considered regarding the calculation of the required number of robotic and automated floor industrial transport. The data for the calculations were provided by a 3pl company that provides warehouse services for Unilever Rus in the city of Tula. Calculations were carried out as part of the as is/to be analysis. For the calculation regarding the stacker robot, current  data  and indicators for 2021 were taken. It should be noted that the figures are averaged, respectively, during the period of high and low seasonality, the data may vary. Calculations were considered for each group of stakeholders at each stage of business processes, where the use of a stacker robot is recommended. Also in this chapter, the ethical question was raised about what to do with workers whose work will be replaced by robotic and automated solutions. Conclusion As part of this final qualification work, value chains and business processes were analyzed, as well as ways to improve warehouse logistics.   Over the past year, the company has increased the salaries of employees by 55%, which entailed additional costs. Moreover, the systemic problem of improving industrial floor transport at the IC warehouse in the city of Tula entails a lack of development in the speed and productivity of pallet handling. The issue of introducing robotic and automated solutions is becoming increasingly relevant, therefore, in order not to lose competitiveness, enterprises must implement tools that will allow them to get higher productivity. To achieve the set goal, which is to offer robotic and automated solutions for the supply chain of Unilever Russia Ice Cream Department, a consistent solution of the following tasks was implemented: Analytical review of the situation in the  warehouse; I ndustry analysis of the ice cream industry in  Russia ; Analy sis of  the current performance of Unilever Rus in the field of sustainable development through the analysis of the value  chain ; Determ ination  the points in the business processes of the warehouse using the analysis of the value chain and business  processes ; Offer ing  technical problem solving  capabilities ; Economic evaluation of recommendations. Chapter 1 was devoted to industry analysis and internal business processes, which helped to understand the trends and position of the company in the market. The analysis showed that the strength of suppliers is very important. Suppliers of technical equipment and their products influence the production capacity and intra-warehouse logistics. Also, 2 main problems were identified, one of which is systemic, and the other is caused by external factors: High costs for warehouse assistance personnel,  taking into account  the increase in the standard of living of employees The lack of productivity of floor industrial transport relative to the possibilities at the technological level. In the second chapter, a value chain analysis was carried out. This tool helped to highlight the main directions of the company, which were later compared with the results of the current warehouse business processes. Thus, the overlay of these analyzes helped to highlight the weak areas in the ice cream warehouse in Tula.   These are areas where inconsistencies were identified from the analysis of the value chain and the real picture in the warehouse : unloading  acceptance  allocation  picking  Automated and robotic solutions were proposed for the respective areas in view of the stacker and loader robot with long forks and the relevance of their application was substantiated. In the third chapter, the results of calculations regarding the required number of purchased and rented industrial vehicles were presented, and the ethical issue regarding t = retraining of personnel replaced by a robot was mentioned.
